g.data-narration: Welcome to Multiple-Processor Scheduling. Learn how modern operating systems efficiently manage tasks across multiple CPUs.
Traditional single-CPU scheduling cannot fully utilize modern multi-core processors, leading to inefficient resource usage.
g.text: Traditional scheduling works for single CPU. Modern systems have
multiple cores → need new strategies to maximize performance!

g.data-narration: Four key concepts: SMP vs ASMP architectures, processor affinity, load balancing, and multicore synchronization.
ASMP uses a master-slave model with one scheduler, while SMP lets each CPU self-schedule with peer equality.
g.text: Each CPU has its own ready queue

g.data-narration: Processor affinity keeps processes on the same CPU to preserve cache data, avoiding costly cache repopulation.
g.text: Scheduler tries to keep process on same CPU

g.data-narration: Load balancing redistributes work from busy CPUs to idle ones using push or pull migration strategies.
Multicore CPUs share caches between cores, allowing cooperating processes to communicate efficiently.
g.text: Schedule cooperating processes on cores sharing cache

g.data-narration: In this example, load balancer detects CPU1 has extra work and migrates P2 to idle CPU2 for better utilization.
SMP dominates modern systems with self-scheduling CPUs, while processor affinity and load balancing optimize performance together.
g.text: SMP is the standard - each CPU self-schedules
Load balancing ensures no CPU is idle
Multicore: schedule cooperating processes on shared-cache cores
Balance affinity vs load balancing carefully
